verses.flow: Now it came about after the death of Moses the servant of the LORD, that the LORD spoke to Joshua the son of Nun, Moses' assistant, saying,
verses.note: TRANSITION: The phrase 'after the death of Moses' links Joshua directly to Deuteronomy 34. The title 'servant of the LORD' (eved YHWH) is the highest honorific in the OT -- Moses held it; Joshua will only receive it at his death (Josh 24:29). Joshua is called 'assistant' (mesharet), the word used for priests serving in the tabernacle and for angels ministering before God (Psalm 104:4).

verses.flow: "Moses My servant is dead. Now therefore arise, cross over this Jordan, you and all this people, to the land which I am giving to them, to the sons of Israel.
verses.note: HEBREW: 'Moses My servant is dead' -- three words in Hebrew (Mosheh avdi met) of devastating finality. 'Now therefore arise' (ve'attah qum) shifts immediately from mourning to mission. God does not allow prolonged grief to delay His purposes.

verses.flow: Every place on which the sole of your foot treads, I have given it to you, just as I spoke to Moses.
verses.note: PROPHETIC PERFECT: 'I have given it to you' (netattivu) -- the past tense for a future event. In Hebrew, this construction indicates an action so certain in God's decree that it is spoken of as already accomplished. The land is already theirs in the divine council's decision; the conquest simply actualizes what God has determined.

verses.flow: From the wilderness and this Lebanon, even as far as the great river, the river Euphrates, all the land of the Hittites, and as far as the Great Sea toward the setting of the sun, shall be your territory.
verses.note: GEOGRAPHY: The boundaries match the Abrahamic covenant (Gen 15:18-21) -- from the wilderness (Negev) and Lebanon in the north, to the Euphrates in the east, to the Great Sea (Mediterranean) in the west. 'The land of the Hittites' may refer to the Hittite Empire's former Syro-Canaanite territories, or to the Hittite populations within Canaan. This is the maximum extent of the promised land, only partially achieved under David and Solomon.

verses.note: CHRIST TYPOLOGY: 'I will not fail you or forsake you' -- quoted in Hebrews 13:5 as a promise to every believer through Christ. What was spoken to one man (Joshua) becomes universal through the greater Joshua (Jesus). The Hebrew verbs raphah (fail/let go) and azav (forsake/abandon) are emphatic negations -- God will absolutely never release His grip or walk away.

The word zonah (prostitute) is the same word the prophets use for spiritual unfaithfulness -- a literal Canaanite zonah proves more faithful than Israel will be.

verses.flow: unless, when we come into the land, you tie this cord of scarlet thread in the window through which you let us down, and gather to yourself into the house your father and your mother and your brothers and all your father's household.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The 'cord of scarlet thread' (tikvat chut hashani) parallels the Passover blood on the doorposts (Exod 12:7, 13). The Hebrew word tikvah means both 'cord' and 'hope.' Rahab's scarlet cord is literally her 'scarlet hope.' The early church father Clement of Rome (~96 AD) identified the scarlet cord as a type of redemption through Christ's blood (1 Clement 12:7-8). Those inside the cord-marked house live; those outside die -- exactly the Passover pattern.

verses.flow: And he said, "No; rather I indeed come now as commander of the army of the LORD." And Joshua fell on his face to the earth and bowed down, and said to him, "What does my lord say to his servant?"
verses.note: KEY DIVINE COUNCIL TEXT: 'Commander of the army of the LORD' (sar tseva YHWH) -- a throne-room title. The tseva YHWH is the heavenly host that stands in YHWH's throne room (1 Kings 22:19). This figure leads the angelic army. His answer 'No' (lo) is theologically critical: the Commander does not serve Israel's agenda; Israel serves the Commander's agenda. The conquest is YHWH's war, not Israel's. Joshua's prostration (yishtachu -- worship) is accepted without rebuke, unlike Revelation 19:10 where the angel refuses worship. CHRIST TYPOLOGY: Patristic interpreters (Eusebius, Origen, Theodoret) widely identified this figure as a pre-incarnate appearance of Christ. Hebrews 2:10 calls Jesus the 'pioneer (archegos) of salvation' -- the NT equivalent of the sar tseva YHWH. See also Revelation 19:11-16, where Christ leads the armies of heaven with a sword.

verses.note: BURNING BUSH PARALLEL: 'Remove your sandals from your feet, for the place where you are standing is holy' -- the exact words of Exodus 3:5, where YHWH Himself appeared to Moses in the burning bush. The chapter ends abruptly after Joshua obeys -- the narrative continues seamlessly into the Jericho instructions (chapter 6), suggesting the Commander's battle plan IS the Jericho plan. The Commander of heaven's army has come to direct the conquest personally.

verses.flow: And the city shall be under the ban, it and all that is in it belongs to the LORD; only Rahab the prostitute and all who are with her in the house shall live, because she hid the messengers whom we sent.
verses.note: HEREM: 'Under the ban' (cherem) -- devoted to YHWH for destruction. Everything that breathes is killed; only metals go to YHWH's treasury. The herem transforms the city into a sacred offering. COMMONLY MISUNDERSTOOD: The herem is not genocide motivated by ethnic hatred. It is a specific divine command for a specific situation, presented as an act of divine judgment (cf. Gen 15:16, where God delays judgment on the Amorites for 400 years). The herem is never generalized as a model for subsequent warfare.

Matthew 1:5 places her in the genealogy of Jesus: 'Salmon the father of Boaz by Rahab.' verses.flow: Then Joshua spoke to the LORD in the day when the LORD gave the Amorites over before the sons of Israel, and he said in the sight of Israel, "Sun, be still over Gibeon, and moon, over the valley of Aijalon!"
verses.note: The sun standing still — the Hebrew 'damam' means 'to be silent, to cease, to stand still.' Joshua does not pray quietly; he commands the cosmos 'in the sight of Israel.' This is the LORD of hosts (armies) fighting for Israel. The sun and moon — worshipped as deities throughout the ancient Near East — are shown to be servants of Israel's God, obedient to the voice of a man who speaks with divine authority. Some scholars interpret this as a poetic way of describing the battle lasting through the day; others take it as a literal cosmic miracle. Either way, the theological point is clear: God commands creation for the sake of His people.

verses.note: The Book of Jasher (Sefer HaYashar, 'Book of the Upright') is referenced as a historical source. It is also cited in 2 Samuel 1:18 (David's lament for Saul and Jonathan). This is one of the 'lost books' — extra-biblical texts cited by Scripture itself. Its existence demonstrates that the biblical authors drew on a broader literary tradition and expected their audience to recognize these sources. The citation formula 'Is this not written in...' implies readers could verify the account.

The remnant of the giants in Philistine territory sets up the David and Goliath narrative generations later. What Joshua left unfinished, David would complete with a sling and a stone.

the king of Aphek, one; the king of Lasharon, one;
the king of Madon, one; the king of Hazor, one;
the king of Shimron-meron, one; the king of Achshaph, one;
the king of Taanach, one; the king of Megiddo, one;
the king of Kedesh, one; the king of Jokneam in Carmel, one;
the king of Dor in the heights of Dor, one; the king of Goiim in Gilgal, one;
the king of Tirzah, one: thirty-one kings in all.
verses.note: Thirty-one kings — the number represents the comprehensive defeat of Canaan's royal power structure. Each 'one' (echad) after each king's name has a cumulative rhetorical effect in the Hebrew: king after king after king, all fallen. The entire political and military infrastructure of Canaan has been dismantled. The repetitive 'one... one... one' reads like the tolling of a bell — a liturgy of divine judgment. Christ typology: as Joshua conquered thirty-one earthly kings, Christ conquered principalities and powers (Colossians 2:15), making a public spectacle of them.

verses.flow: Now Joshua was old and advanced in years, and the LORD said to him, 'You are old and advanced in years, and very much of the land remains to be possessed.'
verses.note: The LORD's statement to aging Joshua is striking in its candor. The Hebrew zaqanta ba'ta bayyamim ('you are old, advanced in days') is the same phrase used of Abraham (Gen 24:1). Much land remains unpossessed -- a theological theme: God grants, but Israel must still walk it out. The tension between divine gift and human responsibility runs through the entire book.

verses.note: Og king of Bashan -- 'the last of the remnant of the Rephaim.' The Rephaim were a race of giants connected to the Nephilim tradition (Gen 6:4, 'also afterward'). Og's territory included 60 fortified cities (Deut 3:4). His iron bedstead measured 9 by 4 cubits (~13.5 x 6 feet, Deut 3:11). The defeat of the Rephaim is a major theme: God systematically dismantles the giant strongholds throughout the conquest.

verses.note: A sobering note: the Geshurites and Maacathites were never expelled. 'To this day' indicates the author writing during a period when these peoples still dwelt in Israel. David later married Maacah, daughter of the king of Geshur (2 Sam 3:3), and their son Absalom fled to Geshur after murdering Amnon.

verses.flow: Only to the tribe of Levi he gave no inheritance; the fire offerings of the LORD, the God of Israel, are their inheritance, as he spoke to them.
verses.note: Levi's 'inheritance' is the LORD Himself and His fire offerings (isheh YHWH). This establishes the priestly economic system: no territorial allotment, but sustained by the tithes and offerings of the other tribes. This is picked up in the NT: 'The Lord is my portion' (Lam 3:24; cf. Ps 16:5, 73:26).

verses.note: Caleb's faith confession: 'Give me this hill country' -- at 85 years old, he asks for the HARDEST territory, where the Anakim giants still live in great fortified cities. This is the opposite of the fear at Kadesh-barnea 45 years earlier. TYPOLOGY: Faith does not ask for the easy path but for the place where God's power is most needed. 'Perhaps the LORD will be with me' -- not doubt but deference (Hebrew ulai, 'if it be that'). Caleb does not presume, yet he advances.

verses.flow: But as for the Jebusites, the inhabitants of Jerusalem, the sons of Judah could not drive them out, and the Jebusites dwell with the sons of Judah in Jerusalem to this day.
verses.note: The critical failure verse: 'The Jebusites dwell with the sons of Judah in Jerusalem to this day.' Judah could not drive out Jerusalem's inhabitants. This failure persisted for ~400 years until David conquered the Jebusite fortress (2 Sam 5:6-7) and made it his capital. Partial obedience has generational consequences. The 'to this day' formula indicates the author wrote before David's conquest, placing at least this portion of Joshua in the pre-Davidic period.

Now Zelophehad the son of Hepher, the son of Gilead, the son of Machir, the son of Manasseh, had no sons but only daughters, and these are the names of his daughters: Mahlah, and Noah, Hoglah, Milcah, and Tirzah.

verses.flow: And Joshua said to them, 'If you are a numerous people, go up to the forest and clear ground for yourself there in the land of the Perizzites and the Rephaim, since the hill country of Ephraim is too narrow for you.'
verses.note: Joshua's response to Joseph's complaint is masterful leadership: 'If you are numerous, go clear the forest.' He does not deny their size but redirects their energy from complaint to action. The Rephaim and Perizzites in the forests were real threats, but Joshua says a great people should welcome great challenges.

verses.note: 'Chariots of iron' (rekev barzel) -- the Canaanite military advantage. Iron technology gave the lowland Canaanites superiority on flat ground. Israel would not master iron-working until the early monarchy period (1 Sam 13:19-22). But God's promise overrides technological disadvantage (cf. Josh 11:6-9, Judg 4:15).

verses.note: The tabernacle moves to Shiloh -- a pivotal moment. Shiloh in the hill country of Ephraim becomes the central sanctuary for the entire pre-monarchic period (~300 years, until its destruction ~1050 BC, 1 Sam 4). 'The land was subdued before them' (nikbeshah ha'aretz) -- the same verb as Gen 1:28 ('subdue the earth'). Conquest fulfills the creation mandate.

verses.flow: And the territory of the sons of Dan went out beyond them, for the sons of Dan went up and fought against Leshem and captured it and struck it with the edge of the sword and took possession of it and dwelt in it, and they called Leshem, Dan, after the name of Dan their father.
verses.note: The pivotal verse for Dan: 'The territory of Dan went out beyond them' -- Hebrew vayyetse gevul benei-Dan mehem. Unable to hold their coastal allotment against Amorite/Philistine pressure (Judg 1:34), the Danites migrated north and conquered Leshem/Laish (Judg 18), renaming it Dan. This northern Dan ('from Dan to Beer-sheba') later became a site of Jeroboam's golden calf idolatry (1 Kings 12:29). Dan's failure to hold their God-given territory led to idolatry at their self-chosen territory.

verses.flow: These are the inheritances which Eleazar the priest and Joshua the son of Nun and the heads of the fathers' houses of the tribes of the sons of Israel distributed by lot at Shiloh before the LORD, at the doorway of the tent of meeting. And they finished dividing the land.
verses.note: The colophon summarizing the entire allotment: Eleazar (priestly authority), Joshua (civil-military authority), and the tribal heads (representative authority) distributed land by lot (divine authority) at Shiloh before the LORD at the tent of meeting. Four layers of authority converge. 'And they finished dividing the land' -- vayekhullu mechallaq et-ha'aretz. The verb kala ('finish/complete') echoes Genesis 2:1 -- 'the heavens and earth were finished.' As creation was completed, so the new creation of Israel's inheritance is completed.

verses.flow: "Speak to the sons of Israel, saying, 'Designate for yourselves the cities of refuge, of which I spoke to you through Moses,
verses.note: CHRIST TYPOLOGY: The cities of refuge point directly to Christ. The manslayer flees to the city for protection from the avenger of blood (the go'el haddam) -- just as the sinner flees to Christ for protection from the law's death penalty (Rom 6:23). The manslayer must remain in the city until the death of the high priest -- Christ our High Priest died once for all (Heb 7:27), and His death opens permanent refuge. Under the old covenant the death of the high priest released the manslayer; under the new covenant the death of THE High Priest releases all who take refuge in Him. Numbers 35:25-28 provides the foundational legislation that Joshua 20 now implements.

verses.flow: that the one who kills a person unintentionally, without knowledge, may flee there; and they shall be for you a refuge from the avenger of blood.
verses.note: HEBREW: bishgagah, 'in error / unintentionally' -- the cities of refuge are specifically for unintentional killing, not premeditated murder. Numbers 35:16-21 defines the distinction. The go'el haddam (avenger of blood) was the nearest male relative obligated to pursue blood justice -- a deeply embedded ANE institution. The cities of refuge tempered blood vengeance with due process.

verses.flow: And he shall dwell in that city until he stands before the congregation for judgment, until the death of the high priest who is in those days; then the one who killed shall return and come to his own city and to his own house, to the city from which he fled.'"
verses.note: The manslayer must dwell in the city of refuge until 'the death of the high priest.' This is one of the most theologically pregnant provisions in the Torah. It binds the release of the guilty to the death of the priestly mediator -- a clear foreshadowing of Christ's atoning death. The 'congregation' (edah) served as the judicial body determining guilt or innocence (Num 35:24-25).

verses.flow: So they set apart Kedesh in Galilee in the hill country of Naphtali, and Shechem in the hill country of Ephraim, and Kiriath-arba -- that is, Hebron -- in the hill country of Judah.
verses.note: Three cities west of the Jordan: Kedesh (Naphtali, in the north), Shechem (Ephraim, in the center), and Hebron (Judah, in the south). The geographic spread ensured that no Israelite was ever too far from refuge. Kiriath-arba is identified as Hebron -- Arba was the greatest man among the Anakim (Josh 14:15). That the city of the giants becomes a city of mercy is a quiet irony.

verses.flow: And beyond the Jordan, east of Jericho, they designated Bezer in the wilderness on the plateau from the tribe of Reuben, and Ramoth in Gilead from the tribe of Gad, and Golan in Bashan from the tribe of Manasseh.
verses.note: Three cities east of the Jordan: Bezer (Reuben), Ramoth in Gilead (Gad), and Golan in Bashan (Manasseh). Six cities total -- three on each side of the Jordan, distributed so that refuge was accessible from any location in the land. Deuteronomy 19:1-13 commanded this arrangement. Moses had already designated the three eastern cities (Deut 4:41-43); Joshua now completes the system.

verses.flow: These were the appointed cities for all the sons of Israel and for the sojourner who sojourns among them, that anyone who kills a person unintentionally may flee there, and not die by the hand of the avenger of blood until he stands before the congregation.
verses.note: The cities of refuge were open to the ger (sojourner/alien) as well as to native Israelites. This universality is significant: God's provision of refuge was not limited by ethnicity. The principle anticipates the gospel's universal offer of salvation -- 'whosoever will may come' (Rev 22:17). Christ is the refuge for all nations.

verses.flow: So the sons of Israel gave to the Levites from their inheritance, according to the command of the LORD, these cities and their pasturelands.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL PRINCIPLE: Every tribe gave cities to the Levites. This ensured that the priestly teaching class was distributed throughout Israel rather than concentrated in one region. The Levites were the LORD's portion (Num 18:20), and their scattering fulfilled Jacob's prophecy of Genesis 49:7 -- 'I will divide them in Jacob and scatter them in Israel.' What was pronounced as a curse on Levi for Shechem's violence was transformed by God's grace into a blessing: the Levites' scattering became the mechanism for teaching the Torah throughout the entire land (Deut 33:10; 2 Chr 17:7-9).

verses.flow: All the cities of the Levites in the midst of the possession of the sons of Israel were forty-eight cities with their pasturelands.
verses.note: Forty-eight cities total for the Levites -- a number that ensured comprehensive coverage of the entire land. The Levitical city system was essentially an educational and worship infrastructure: Torah teachers in every region, accessible to every Israelite. verses.note: Joshua assembles all Israel at Shechem -- the most covenantally significant location in the land. Abraham first received the land promise here (Gen 12:6-7). Jacob buried his household's foreign gods here (Gen 35:2-4). Joshua renewed the covenant here after Ai (Josh 8:30-35). verses.flow: And Joshua said to all the people, "Thus says the LORD, the God of Israel: 'Your fathers dwelt beyond the River from ancient times -- Terah, the father of Abraham and the father of Nahor -- and they served other gods.
verses.note: 'Your fathers dwelt beyond the River from ancient times... and they served other gods.' YHWH's historical prologue begins with a stunning admission: Israel's patriarchal family was pagan. Terah, Abraham's father, worshipped the gods of Mesopotamia. 'Beyond the River' (ever hanahar) means east of the Euphrates -- Ur and Haran. The 'other gods' (elohim acherim) are not denied or dismissed as nonexistent; they are real spiritual beings from whose jurisdiction YHWH extracted Abraham. Joshua 24:2 is one of the Bible's most honest verses about Israel's origins: chosen not because of ancestral piety but despite ancestral idolatry.

verses.flow: And I sent the hornet before you, and it drove them out from before you -- the two kings of the Amorites -- not by your sword and not by your bow.
verses.note: 'I sent the hornet before you.' The tsirah (hornet) appears in Exodus 23:28 and Deuteronomy 7:20 as a divine weapon. Whether literal insects, a metaphor for panic and terror, or a reference to Egyptian military campaigns that weakened Canaan before Israel's arrival, the point is the same: 'not by your sword and not by your bow.' Israel's conquest was accomplished by divine power, not human military might.

verses.flow: And I gave you a land for which you did not labor, and cities which you did not build, and you dwell in them; of vineyards and olive groves which you did not plant, you eat.'
verses.note: 'A land for which you did not labor, cities which you did not build, vineyards and olive groves which you did not plant.' Everything Israel possesses is a gift. This is the theological foundation for the demand that follows: since everything comes from God, serve God alone. Grace precedes law. Gift precedes obligation. This is the structure of every biblical covenant -- and the structure of the gospel (Eph 2:8-10).

verses.flow: "Now therefore, fear the LORD and serve Him in sincerity and in truth; and put away the gods which your fathers served beyond the River and in Egypt, and serve the LORD.
verses.note: 'Put away the gods which your fathers served beyond the River and in Egypt.' The command to 'put away' (hasiru) implies that some Israelites still possessed foreign gods -- household idols, Egyptian amulets, Canaanite figurines. Jacob issued the same command at this same location (Gen 35:2-4). Centuries later, the same idolatrous tendency persists. Ezekiel 20:7-8 confirms that Israel never fully abandoned Egyptian gods.

verses.flow: And if it is evil in your eyes to serve the LORD, choose for yourselves today whom you will serve -- whether the gods which your fathers served that were beyond the River, or the gods of the Amorites in whose land you are dwelling. But as for me and my house, we will serve the LORD."
verses.note: 'Choose for yourselves today whom you will serve... but as for me and my house, we will serve the LORD.' The most famous verse in Joshua. The choice is presented as genuine and urgent: 'today' (hayyom). Joshua names three options: (1) the gods of Mesopotamia, (2) the gods of Canaan, (3) the LORD. He does not argue -- he declares his own decision and demands theirs. THEOLOGICAL SIGNIFICANCE: This verse demonstrates that covenant faithfulness is a conscious choice, not an inherited status. Each generation must choose. Each household must decide. CHRIST CONNECTION: Jesus presents the same binary -- 'No one can serve two masters' (Matt 6:24). Joshua's either/or anticipates Christ's either/or. There is no neutral ground.

verses.flow: Then Joshua said to the people, "You are not able to serve the LORD, for He is a holy God. He is a jealous God; He will not forgive your transgression or your sins.
verses.note: 'You are not able to serve the LORD, for He is a holy God. He is a jealous God; He will not forgive your transgression or your sins.' Joshua's shocking response to the people's pledge. This is NOT saying God never forgives -- the entire sacrificial system exists because He does. Joshua is saying: You cannot serve YHWH casually. You cannot serve Him while keeping your foreign gods. His holiness (qodesh) consumes everything impure. His jealousy (qin'ah) tolerates no rivals. Presumptuous, half-hearted worship of the holy God leads to judgment, not blessing. Only through the coming Messiah's perfect sacrifice would full and final forgiveness be accomplished (Heb 9:12-14; 10:10-14). Joshua is driving the people to understand the weight of their commitment.

verses.flow: Then Joshua said to the people, "You are witnesses against yourselves that you have chosen for yourselves the LORD, to serve Him." And they said, "We are witnesses."
verses.note: 'You are witnesses against yourselves that you have chosen the LORD.' Joshua makes the people self-witnesses -- they testify against themselves that they have voluntarily committed to YHWH. If they later apostatize, they cannot claim ignorance. Their own words condemn them. This is covenant ratification language -- binding, irrevocable, and witnessed.

verses.flow: So Joshua made a covenant with the people that day, and set for them a statute and an ordinance at Shechem.
verses.note: Joshua makes a formal covenant (berit) and establishes 'a statute and an ordinance' (choq umishpat). This follows the ANE suzerainty treaty pattern: (1) preamble (v. 2a), (2) historical prologue (vv. 2b-13), (3) stipulations (vv. 14-24), (4) document deposit (v. 26), (5) witnesses (vv. 26-27). The Shechem covenant is a formal renewal of the Sinai/Moab covenant for a new generation in a new land.

verses.flow: And Joshua wrote these words in the Book of the Law of God; and he took a great stone and set it up there under the oak that was by the sanctuary of the LORD.
verses.note: 'Joshua wrote these words in the Book of the Law of God.' He adds to the sacred written text -- the Torah is not a closed, inaccessible document but a living covenant record. The 'great stone' under the 'oak' (elah, or terebinth) at the sanctuary serves as a permanent witness marker. Standing stones as covenant witnesses are well attested throughout the ANE (cf. Gen 28:18; 31:44-52; Exod 24:4).

verses.flow: And Joshua said to all the people, "Behold, this stone shall be a witness against us, for it has heard all the words of the LORD which He spoke with us; so it shall be a witness against you, lest you deny your God."
verses.note: 'This stone has heard all the words of the LORD which He spoke with us.' The personification of the stone as a hearing witness echoes Deuteronomy 30:19 and 31:28, where Moses calls heaven and earth as witnesses. In the ANE, deities and cosmic elements were standard covenant witnesses. Here a stone assumes that function -- silent, enduring, immovable testimony to the covenant commitment.

verses.flow: And it came to pass after these things that Joshua the son of Nun, the servant of the LORD, died, being one hundred and ten years old.
verses.note: Joshua dies at 110 years old -- the same age as Joseph (Gen 50:26). In Egyptian tradition, 110 was the ideal lifespan, the age of perfect blessing. Joshua receives the supreme title: 'the servant of the LORD' (eved YHWH) -- the same title held by Moses (Josh 1:1). Joshua earned this title at death, not during life. He finished the race. He kept the faith.

verses.flow: And Israel served the LORD all the days of Joshua and all the days of the elders who survived Joshua, and who had known all the work of the LORD which He had done for Israel.
verses.note: 'Israel served the LORD all the days of Joshua and all the days of the elders who survived Joshua.' Faithfulness lasted exactly one generation -- the generation that had seen God's works. The generation that only heard about them abandoned the covenant (Judg 2:10-12). This is the perpetual danger: secondhand faith does not hold. Every generation must encounter God for itself.

verses.flow: And the bones of Joseph, which the sons of Israel brought up from Egypt, they buried at Shechem, in the piece of ground which Jacob had bought from the sons of Hamor the father of Shechem for one hundred pieces of silver; and they became an inheritance for the sons of Joseph.
verses.note: Joseph's bones, carried out of Egypt at the Exodus (Exod 13:19), are finally buried at Shechem -- in the very plot of ground Jacob purchased (Gen 33:19). Joseph's dying wish (Gen 50:25) is fulfilled after more than 400 years. The bones traveled through the Red Sea, through 40 years of wilderness wandering, across the Jordan, through the conquest -- and now rest in the Promised Land. GOD KEEPS HIS PROMISES ACROSS GENERATIONS. No promise of God expires. No oath of God is forgotten. The burial of Joseph's bones is the quiet, definitive proof of God's faithfulness to His word.

verses.flow: And Eleazar the son of Aaron died, and they buried him in the hill of Phinehas his son, which had been given to him in the hill country of Ephraim.
verses.note: Eleazar the son of Aaron dies -- the end of the priestly leader who served alongside Joshua throughout the conquest and allotment. He is buried 'in the hill of Phinehas his son' in the hill country of Ephraim. The book ends with three burials: Joshua (the commander), Joseph (the patriarch), Eleazar (the priest). The conquest generation passes. The land is settled. The covenant has been renewed. What comes next -- the book of Judges -- will reveal whether the Shechem commitment holds. It does not.
